title: Integers and Absolute Value
why: Integers and absolute value sit on a number line. To get them, you need to count steps from 0 in both directions and understand opposites (left/right). Warm up the number line and "how far from zero" feels natural.

diagnostic.q: How many steps is 5 from 0 on a number line?
Which direction is the opposite of moving right?

learn.heading: How far from zero?
learn.intro: On a number line, numbers to the right of 0 are positive and numbers to the left are negative. Absolute value just means: how many steps away from 0 — it is never negative.
examples: Distance from 0: the number 4 is 4 steps right of 0, so its distance is 4.
Opposites: right and left are opposite directions. 6 steps right and 6 steps left are the same distance: 6.

tiers.intro: Count steps from zero, one at a time.

items.q: A. How many steps from 0 to the number 3 (moving right)?
B. How many steps from 0 to the number 7?
C. How many steps from 0 to the number 0?
items.hint: You are already at 0, so 0 steps.

tiers.intro: Distance is always counted as steps.

items.q: A. Which is the opposite direction of left?
B. A frog jumps 6 steps left of 0, then counts how far it is from 0. What is the distance?
items.hint: Distance from 0 is just the number of steps: 6.

items.q: A. Point A is 9 steps right of 0. Point B is at 0. How far apart are they?
A. Which is farther from 0: a point 4 steps left, or a point 6 steps right?
items.hint: Compare steps: 6 > 4, so 6 steps right is farther.

exit.q: 1. How many steps from 0 is the number 7?
exit.hint: Distance from 0 is the number of steps: 7.

exit.q: 2. The opposite of moving 5 steps right is moving 5 steps ___?
exit.hint: Left is the opposite of right.
